faqs.a: Hyperarousal is a sustained state of being keyed up and on guard, as if danger is always near. The nervous system, built to ramp up in an emergency and then settle, gets stuck in the ramped-up position. It's a core feature of PTSD.

faqs.q: What are the signs of hyperarousal?
faqs.a: Common signs include feeling constantly on edge, startling easily, trouble sleeping, difficulty concentrating, and irritability. Physically it can show up as a racing heart, muscle tension, and a wired feeling, as if an emergency is underway.

Yes. Hyperarousal is treatable. Trauma-focused therapies and other approaches can help the nervous system learn to stand down. If it follows a traumatic event and is disrupting daily life, earlier care tends to help.
